channel.title: Scholarly Publishing News - Curated Feed
channel.description: AI-curated news about tools, technology, and partnerships in scholarly publishing

item.title: Guest Post — The SSP Annual Meeting: Keeping the Faith in Unsettled Times
item.description: Today, co-chairs for SSP's 48th Annual Meeting Planning Committee discuss what they're most excited to deliver in this year's program. The post Guest Post — The SSP Annual Meeting: Keeping the Faith in Unsettled Times appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Ask the Librarians: Recapping a Scholarly Kitchen Roundtable at the 2025 Charleston Library Conference
item.description: Today's post recaps a lively roundtable conversation with library and information science experts who have been guest bloggers for TSK and active SSP participants. The post Ask the Librarians: Recapping a Scholarly Kitchen Roundtable at the 2025 Charleston Library Conference appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: This Week’s Texted Discoveries
item.description: We are constantly sharing relevant stuff we find — here’s this week’s fetch

item.title: AI Rollout Is a People Problem: A Pulse on All Things AI, Part 2
item.description: Faced with technological shifts not seen since the advent of the internet, Todd Toler and Angela Cochran posit that the biggest challenges for organizations building an AI strategy are human, not technology. The post AI Rollout Is a People Problem: A Pulse on All Things AI, Part 2 appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Dyslexia Is Not Gastrointestinal
item.description: The stochastic parrot of OE can be easily led down the garden path with words

item.title: Standing Up, Standing Proud, Standing Together: Inside the Pathways to Inclusive Publishing Summit and the Movement for Equity in Scholarly Publishing: Part 3
item.description: Part 3 of a look at the American Society of Civil Engineers’ inaugural Pathways to Inclusive Publishing Summit, which brought together industry leaders, content creators, and allies to explore strategies for fostering inclusivity and accessibility within the publishing ecosystem. The post Standing Up, Standing Proud, Standing Together: Inside the Pathways to Inclusive Publishing Summit and the Movement for Equity in Scholarly Publishing: Part 3 appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — Knowledge as Civic Infrastructure: A Conversation with Nadim Sadek
item.description: Wendy Queen interviews Nadim Sadek. Nadim is a creative strategist and founder of Shimmr AI, who argues that AI can strengthen human creativity rather than replace it. The post Guest Post — Knowledge as Civic Infrastructure: A Conversation with Nadim Sadek appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: The OA Movement Made MAHA
item.description: From conspiracies to commercial citation stacking, the evidence is compelling

item.title: Standing Up, Standing Proud, Standing Together: Inside the Pathways to Inclusive Publishing Summit and the Movement for Equity in Scholarly Publishing: Part 2
item.description: Part 2 of a look at the American Society of Civil Engineers’ inaugural Pathways to Inclusive Publishing Summit, which brought together industry leaders, content creators, and allies to explore strategies for fostering inclusivity and accessibility within the publishing ecosystem. The post Standing Up, Standing Proud, Standing Together: Inside the Pathways to Inclusive Publishing Summit and the Movement for Equity in Scholarly Publishing: Part 2 appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — Diamond Open Access: A Lifeline for the Monograph?
item.description: Today's guest bloggers ask: Can Diamond OA serve as a genuine lifeline for the scholarly monograph? The post Guest Post — Diamond Open Access: A Lifeline for the Monograph? appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Standing Up, Standing Proud, Standing Together: Inside the Pathways to Inclusive Publishing Summit and the Movement for Equity in Scholarly Publishing: Part 1
item.description: Part 1 of a look at the American Society of Civil Engineers’ inaugural Pathways to Inclusive Publishing Summit, which brought together industry leaders, content creators, and allies to explore strategies for fostering inclusivity and accessibility within the publishing ecosystem The post Standing Up, Standing Proud, Standing Together: Inside the Pathways to Inclusive Publishing Summit and the Movement for Equity in Scholarly Publishing: Part 1 appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: What AI Asks of Open Access
item.description: As AI systems increasingly reason from the scientific literature, the integrity signals that make research trustworthy -- open data, structured metadata, robust retraction processes -- matter more than ever. PLOS CEO Alison Mudditt on why open access publishers have a different set of obligations in an AI world. The post What AI Asks of Open Access appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: The “AI Scientist” Hides Money
item.description: Why will nobody divulge who paid to publish this commercial OA paper?

item.title: Guest Post — Senior Librarians as Publisher Change Agents: What’s the Business Case? (Part 2)
item.description: Today's guest blogger continues the conversation about Library Relations roles and what it means to sit at the intersection of libraries and publishing. The post Guest Post — Senior Librarians as Publisher Change Agents: What’s the Business Case? (Part 2) appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: The Tech Fantasy Is Ending
item.description: Juries and funders are burying exploitative models — exorcising ghosts is next

item.title: How Can Scholarly Publishing Overcome Its ‘SDG Inertia’?
item.description: Is there an inertia slowing efforts by scholarly publishers toward achieving the UN's Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)? The post How Can Scholarly Publishing Overcome Its ‘SDG Inertia’? appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Wikipedia Bans AI-Generated Content
item.description: “In recent months, more and more administrative reports centered on LLM-related issues, and editors were being overwhelmed.”

item.title: Guest Post — Senior Librarians as Publisher Change Agents: What’s the Business Case? (Part 1)
item.description: Today's guest blogger discusses Library Relations roles within publishing organizations and asks, what do both publishers and librarians hope for from these appointments? The post Guest Post — Senior Librarians as Publisher Change Agents: What’s the Business Case? (Part 1) appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Scholarly Society Sustainability in an Unstable Publishing World: Reasons to be Cheerful, Parts 1, 2, and 3.
item.description: In this post, Robert attempts to embrace a gloomy optimism as he muses on the state of publishing at scholarly societies. The post Scholarly Society Sustainability in an Unstable Publishing World: Reasons to be Cheerful, Parts 1, 2, and 3. appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: The Role of Gossip in Scholarly Publishing
item.description: Let’s say the quiet part out loud: gossip plays an important role in scholarly publishing. But is that a bad thing? The post The Role of Gossip in Scholarly Publishing appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — All the Seats at the Table: A Summary and Status Review of the NIH APC Caps Proposal
item.description: Today's post considers the NIH proposal to implement APC funding caps, public responses it engendered, and, while we await a final decision, and shares thoughts on what may come next. The post Guest Post — All the Seats at the Table: A Summary and Status Review of the NIH APC Caps Proposal appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: STM Plants a Flag About Responsible Use of Research Content in GenAI
item.description: A new STM Association paper seeks to foster a discussion about how GenAI systems can reliably incorporate scholarly research. The post STM Plants a Flag About Responsible Use of Research Content in GenAI appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — Could AI Help Fix Peer Review, or Will it Only Make Things Worse?
item.description: Today's post asserts that peer review, which is still of vital importance to science, is clearly failing in the current age -- could AI save the day? The post Guest Post — Could AI Help Fix Peer Review, or Will it Only Make Things Worse? appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Did OE Just Admit Defeat?
item.description: And that they’re taking an additional path to extraction and exploitation?

item.title: AI in Peer Review: Revisiting an 8-year-old Debate
item.description: In 2018 at SSP New Directions, Neil Blair Christensen and Angela Cochran participated in an Oxford debate on the use of AI in Peer Review. Today, they revisit their main points and reflect on where they think we are today and will likely be in another 8 years. The post AI in Peer Review: Revisiting an 8-year-old Debate appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Bespoke Preprint Servers Fade
item.description: Cornell pushes arXiv out of the nest to fight openRxiv’s weaklings for food

item.title: PMC and a Withdrawn Preprint
item.description: Nothing means anything in the libertine journals world we’ve enabled

item.title: “Not Great Science”
item.description: The EIC of the JAMA Network elaborates on a bad paper, adding fuel to the fire

item.title: What Publishing Leaders Say About AI When They’re Not on Panels: A Pulse on All Things AI
item.description: Today's post explores issues facing scholarly publishers around AI — using it, layering it, competing against it, and licensing to it. The post What Publishing Leaders Say About AI When They’re Not on Panels: A Pulse on All Things AI appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — Societies 2030: The Community Advantage in an AI-First World
item.description: Today's guest bloggers call for society publishers to recognize their unique role in shaping the systems researchers use to discover and evaluate knowledge. The post Guest Post — Societies 2030: The Community Advantage in an AI-First World appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — The Perils of Using Generative AI to Perform Research Tasks: Editors’ and Publishers’ Viewpoints
item.description: Today's guest post offers a review of a panel of publishers and editors discussing the pros and cons of using Generative AI, along with ethical and policy implications. The post Guest Post — The Perils of Using Generative AI to Perform Research Tasks: Editors’ and Publishers’ Viewpoints appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — Who Owns Our Knowledge? An African University Press Perspective
item.description: Today's guest post asks readers to reckon with the idea that knowledge reflects power, and the global knowledge economy excludes the Global South. The post Guest Post — Who Owns Our Knowledge? An African University Press Perspective appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Pod: Why Should We Trust You?
item.description: Trust markers are being abused to put user-targeted ads into the point of care

item.title: AI Translations Are Adding ‘Hallucinations’ to Wikipedia Articles
item.description: AI translated articles swapped sources or added unsourced sentences with no explanation, while others added paragraphs sourced from completely unrelated material.

item.title: Guest Post — The Value Challenge in Scholarly Publishing
item.description: Today's guest blogger identifies signals of how fractured the scholarly research ecosystem has become, and how the value publishers provide is increasingly questioned, dismissed, or overlooked by key stakeholders. The post Guest Post — The Value Challenge in Scholarly Publishing appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Economic Outlook for Scholarly Communications in 2026 — SSP Pulse Check Report
item.description: SSP's second Pulse Check survey results paint a picture of an industry in defensive mode — cautious, structurally stressed, but not in freefall. The post Economic Outlook for Scholarly Communications in 2026 — SSP Pulse Check Report appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: OpenEvidence Targets Users
item.description: A hot medical LLM relies on a revenue model known to corrupt

item.title: Keeping Knowledge Connected – at PIDfest 2026!
item.description: PIDfest is back and you're invited! Find out more in today's post by Alice Meadows about PIDfest 2026 (October 27-29, Leiden, The Netherlands). The post Keeping Knowledge Connected – at PIDfest 2026! appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Pod: Insurgents vs. Incumbents
item.description: From MAHA to Sense About Science, there’s a new kind of upside-down here

item.title: Guest Post — AI Readiness and the New Value Equation in Scholarly Publishing
item.description: Today's guest bloggers explain how semantic enrichment of scholarly content allows publishers to shape the next generation of technology by making it indispensable to AI. The post Guest Post — AI Readiness and the New Value Equation in Scholarly Publishing appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Number Go Down (NSFW)
item.description: As economic alarms blare, scientific publishing is an AI-fixated sitting duck

item.title: Guest Post — Re-imagining Scholarly Integrity: The “Continuum of Consensus” Quality Control System
item.description: Today's guest blogger proposes the "Continuum of Consensus" as a solution to shore up research integrity, peer review, and the public trust in scholarly research. The post Guest Post — Re-imagining Scholarly Integrity: The “Continuum of Consensus” Quality Control System appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — Diamond Open Access Needs Institutions, Not Heroes
item.description: Today's guest blogger asks: What would it mean to support community-led publishing as infrastructure, rather than as a collection of heroic individual efforts? The post Guest Post — Diamond Open Access Needs Institutions, Not Heroes appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: From “AI helps me write” to “AI runs the workflow”: Eight Tech-trend Reports through a Publishing-and-learning Lens
item.description: A review of eight technology industry trend reports that offer a similar conclusion: AI is no longer a feature. It’s becoming infrastructure — and the unit of value is moving from “a better tool” to “a better system.” The post From “AI helps me write” to “AI runs the workflow”: Eight Tech-trend Reports through a Publishing-and-learning Lens appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — There’s an Elephant in the Room, but Not in Your Usage Reports
item.description: Today's guest bloggers spotlight a gap in traditional usage reporting, third-party AI usage, and recommend steps needed to recover missing usage data. The post Guest Post — There’s an Elephant in the Room, but Not in Your Usage Reports appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: So… IS the Essence of a Journal Portable? Checking in on _NeuroImage_ and _Imaging Neuroscience_
item.description: How are two competing neuroscience journals faring since the editorial board of one departed to create the other? The post So… IS the Essence of a Journal Portable? Checking in on _NeuroImage_ and _Imaging Neuroscience_ appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — Putting the “U” in FAIR
item.description: Today's guest blogger calls for adding "understandable" to the FAIR data principles, to ensure we do not surrender human knowledge in our rush for automation. The post Guest Post — Putting the “U” in FAIR appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Guest Post — Why Science Communication Must be the Next Competitive Edge for Scholarly Publishers
item.description: Today's guest bloggers assert that the future of the scholarly publishing depends on mastering science communication with the same rigor that global consumer brands apply to marketing. The post Guest Post — Why Science Communication Must be the Next Competitive Edge for Scholarly Publishers appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Back to the (Article of the) Future: An interview with Sami Benchekroun and Rod Cookson
item.description: In this interview with Alice Meadows, Sami Benchekroun (Morressier/Molecular Connections) and Rod Cookson (The Royal Society) share their thoughts about how and why scholarly publishing needs to move away from being article-based. The post Back to the (Article of the) Future: An interview with Sami Benchekroun and Rod Cookson appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .

item.title: Responding to the Threat of Zero-Click Search and AI Summaries: How Do We Tame The Crocodile?
item.description: AI-driven zero-click search is widening the gap between visibility and usage, threatening publisher revenue, research integrity, and trust. How should we respond? The post Responding to the Threat of Zero-Click Search and AI Summaries: How Do We Tame The Crocodile? appeared first on The Scholarly Kitchen .
